serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of bong snail stir-fried with garlic butter is approximately 250 g, typically shared as an appetizer or side dish in a Vietnamese meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Snails, raw (FDC ID 175177) and Butter, salted (FDC ID 173410); composite estimation for Vietnamese garlic butter stir-fried snail dish
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese bong snail stir-fried with garlic butter exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for raw snails (FDC ID 175177) and salted butter (FDC ID 173410), weighted to reflect the dish composition (approximately 65% snail meat, 20% butter, 10% garlic and aromatics, 5% fish sauce and seasoning). Input record values (energy 192 kcal, protein 11.2 g, fat 15.2 g, carbohydrate 3.2 g, sodium 380 mg) are broadly consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Calcium 24 mg, iron 1.2 mg, cholesterol 48 mg, saturated fat 7.2 g, trans fat 0.2 g, and vitamin C 2.0 mg are also consistent with the USDA composite and are retained.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate for very-low-carbohydrate shellfish dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese bong snail stir-fried with garlic butter. Carbohydrate content is very low at 3.2 g/100 g (predominantly from garlic and trace fish sauce sugars). GI is estimated at approximately 5 by analogy with other very-low-carbohydrate shellfish and seafood preparations documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (5 × 8.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.4, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 380 mg/100 g from fish sauce and added salt, combined with saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g from butter; insufficient potassium and fibre offset under DASH criteria
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its borderline-high sodium content (380 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat load; if consumed, request preparation with reduced fish sauce, no added salt, and substitution of butter with a small amount of olive oil to lower both sodium and saturated fat burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g) and low sugar (1.2 g/100 g) with high protein (11.2 g/100 g) supporting glycaemic stability; however, high saturated fat (7.2 g/100 g) may worsen insulin resistance with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish poses minimal acute glycaemic risk due to its very low carbohydrate and sugar content; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size and frequency of consumption given the high saturated fat content from butter, which can impair insulin sensitivity over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g and trans fat 0.2 g/100 g from butter, combined with sodium 380 mg/100 g and cholesterol 48 mg/100 g, present a composite cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its high saturated fat (7.2 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) content from butter, which raise LDL cholesterol; sodium at 380 mg/100 g further elevates blood pressure risk, and a 250 g serving would deliver approximately 95 mg cholesterol — request preparation with olive oil substitution and reduced fish sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g from butter exceeds the 5 g/100 g pro-inflammatory threshold; butter is an animal-derived saturated fat without MUFA/PUFA carve-out eligibility
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its standard preparation due to the high saturated fat content from butter (7.2 g/100 g), which promotes systemic inflammation; substitution with extra-virgin olive oil and retention of garlic and Vietnamese coriander would substantially reduce the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bong Snail Stir-Fried with Garlic Butter (Vietnamese: Ốc Bống Xào Bơ Tỏi) is a Vietnamese seafood dish in which bong snails — a small freshwater or estuarine snail variety popular in Vietnamese street food and casual dining — are stir-fried at high heat in a generous quantity of butter with minced garlic, fish sauce, chilli, and Vietnamese coriander (rau răm). The dish is characterised by a rich, aromatic, and savoury flavour profile with a pronounced garlic-butter fragrance, a slightly briny depth from the fish sauce, and a mild heat from chilli. The snail meat is tender and slightly chewy with a clean oceanic taste. It is commonly served as an appetizer or a shared side dish in Vietnamese restaurants and street food settings, often accompanied by bread or eaten directly from the shell using a toothpick. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 192 kcal, 11.2 g protein, 15.2 g fat (of which 7.2 g is saturated fat from butter), 3.2 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g sugar, 0.4 g dietary fibre, 380 mg sodium, 24 mg calcium, 1.2 mg iron, 48 mg cholesterol, 0.2 g trans fat, and 2.0 mg vitamin C. The high saturated fat and sodium content make this dish a moderate-to-high concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis, while its very low carbohydrate content makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation. The dish is not suitable for vegans, vegetarians, or those with shellfish or dairy allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Vietnamese bong snail stir-fried with garlic butter exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for raw snails and salted butter, weighted by approximate dish composition. Input record values from the GrabFood-sourced AI-generated record are broadly consistent with this composite and are retained at medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate for a very-low-carbohydrate shellfish preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for Vietnamese bong snail stir-fried with garlic butter is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16; the composite dish-level fatty acid breakdown cannot be reliably derived without laboratory analysis. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation is not reported in any approved source consulted. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Vietnamese dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16. All core macros (protein_g 11.2, fat_g 15.2, carbohydrate_g 3.2) are non-zero and consistent with the USDA composite estimate for a snail-and-butter stir-fry. Input record had duplicate portion entries (two identical '1 plate / 250 g' entries); deduplicated to a single serving of 250 g. The dish is sold in Singapore Vietnamese restaurants and food delivery platforms but originates from Vietnamese culinary tradition; country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore, reflecting the point-of-sale context rather than culinary origin — this record uses Vietnam as the region of origin. Halal status is uncertain as the dish may contain pork lard in some preparations; is_halal set to false as a conservative default.
